key_reasoning: Outside view: formal public bilateral oil-for-yuan agreements with explicit volume commitments have a near-zero historical base rate in any 30-day window; no state pair has achieved this format on that timeline even under sustained strategic pressure. China's unprecedented sanctions defiance order (Day 65) is the strongest positive signal and warrants a Bayesian update of roughly 2× above base, but a compliance directive to domestic firms is categorically different from a formal public agreement inviting maximum US secondary-sanctions targeting. The Hormuz yuan payment infrastructure ('at least two vessels paid in yuan') shows informal yuanization is advancing, but the scenario explicitly excludes these crude-swap and informal-credit arrangements. The binding constraint is China's structural preference for deniable informality: announcing explicit volume commitments publicly would expose Chinese entities to US sanctions enforcement in a way the compliance directive does not, and would directly undermine China's positioning as a neutral ceasefire mediator (Wang Yi–Araghchi meeting, Day 69). Fermi decomposition: P(China willing to formalize and publicize) ≈ 0.12 × P(Iran willing) ≈ 0.65 × P(terms agreed in 30 days if both willing) ≈ 0.45 × P(explicit volume commitment included) ≈ 0.35 ≈ 0.012; adjusted upward to 0.04 to account for the unprecedented China sanctions escalation and Trump–Xi summit pressure on 14–15 May as a possible catalyst for a provocative Chinese signal, but the formal/public/volume threshold remains the load-bearing constraint that base rates and China's diplomatic tradition both drive toward the low end.

framework_specific.outside_view: Base rate for formal public bilateral oil-for-yuan agreements with explicit volume commitments in any 30-day window: ~2–3%. No historical precedent for this specific format in crisis conditions.
framework_specific.bayesian_chain: Prior 3% → China sanctions defiance order LR 2.0 → 6% → Araghchi–Wang Yi meeting LR 1.3 → 7.8% → Informal yuan payments existing LR 1.2 → 9.4% → China mediator positioning conflict LR 0.6 → 5.6% → Formal/public requirement vs China preferences LR 0.4 → 2.2% → 30-day window constraint LR 0.7 → 1.5% → Explicit volume commitment requirement LR 0.7 → 1.1%; adjusted upward to 4% for Trump–Xi summit wildcard and unprecedented sanctions defiance signal
framework_specific.fermi_decomposition: P(China willing to formalize and publicize) ~12% × P(Iran willing) ~65% × P(terms agreed in 30 days given both willing) ~45% × P(explicit volume commitment included) ~35% = ~1.2%; adjusted to 4% for unprecedented China escalation posture
anti_bias_flags: Availability bias risk: China's sanctions defiance order is dramatic but a compliance directive is categorically different from a formal public bilateral agreement — resisting the temptation to conflate them
Scope insensitivity check: informal yuan payments already occurring; the scenario's exclusion of these is the key threshold — the formal/public/volume requirement is genuinely novel
Anchoring check: 4% is not a round number and reflects the specific threshold conditions rather than generic 'China-Iran deal' probability
key_discriminators: Whether Trump–Xi summit (14–15 May) produces a breakdown prompting China to make a provocative formal signal
Whether Iran's Hormuz toll regime formalises further into a publicly announced bilateral framework
Whether China shifts from deniable compliance directive to explicit public commitment model for Iran oil
framework_specific.dissent: The argument for a higher probability rests on the unprecedented China sanctions defiance signal and the trajectory of yuanization of Hormuz payments. If this is read as the beginning of a deliberate Chinese campaign to formalise economic support for Iran under the conflict umbrella, the probability could reach 8–10%. However, the formal/public/explicit-volume threshold is specifically designed to exclude the informal arrangements that are more likely to expand, and China's demonstrated preference for deniability is a strong structural constraint.
